primer.markdown:
# Register once; invoke like any other tool

A server is only useful when a client knows how to find it. Claude Code has one command for that — `claude mcp add <name> -- <launch-command>` — and one config file it writes to: `~/.claude.json`. The entry stores a human name, the process launch line (often `node dist/server.js` for TypeScript or `python -m my_mcp` for Python), and an optional `env` block for anything secret. That is the whole registration surface.

Once registered, the server participates in a Claude Code session exactly the way a `/meta → /rmp → /review` pipeline does in `docs/EXAMPLES.md`: the client composes the server's capabilities into the chain. When Claude decides a tool is appropriate, it sends `tools/call` down the registered transport, receives the typed result, and splices it into the conversation. The wiring is declarative at setup, automatic at runtime.

When the transport refuses to speak — and it will, the first time — the debug loop is small and mechanical. Check `claude mcp list` to confirm the server is registered. Check the process is actually launchable from the exact path in `~/.claude.json` (absolute paths beat shell aliases every time). Tail the server's stderr while Claude Code boots to catch `initialize` failures. If the handshake completes but no tools appear, your `ListToolsRequestSchema` handler is not registered — or your `capabilities` bag never mentioned `tools`. Three places to look; one of them is always the cause.
primer.key_insight: `claude mcp add` is one command that writes one JSON entry into `~/.claude.json`. When the handshake fails, the cause is almost always the launch path, the capability bag, or the handler registration — in that order.

visual.alt: A Claude Code window on the left with a conversation panel. An arrow labelled `claude mcp add my-server -- node dist/server.js` writes into a central `~/.claude.json` box showing a `mcpServers` entry. A second arrow from that entry launches a subprocess on the right representing the MCP server, linked back to the Claude Code window by a bidirectional stdio pipe carrying JSON-RPC frames. A small debug panel underneath lists three failure modes: wrong launch path, missing tools capability, unregistered handler.

sandbox_policy.csp: default-src 'none'; style-src 'unsafe-inline'; script-src 'unsafe-inline'; img-src data:; font-src https://fonts.gstatic.com; connect-src 'none'; frame-ancestors 'self'

interactive.success_criterion: Learner types `claude mcp add my-server -- node dist/server.js` into the simulator, sees the generated `~/.claude.json` patch, then simulates a session by clicking `invoke tool`. If the learner intentionally introduces a capability mismatch (toggle `tools` off), the simulator shows the resulting `method not found` error; after re-enabling `tools`, the invocation succeeds. The success pill appears once both the success and the failure path have been observed.

artifact.excerpt:
# Examples

Real recipes that use the five commands together.

---

## 1. Ship a feature end-to-end

**Goal:** implement a feature, iterate until good, then review before merge.

```
/chain [/meta→/rmp→/review] @quality:0.9 "add rate limiting to /api/login with exponential backoff"
```

- `/meta` picks a strategy based on complexity (likely L4 — algorithm + integration).
- `/rmp` iterates until the implementation scores ≥ 0.9 on the quality rubric.
- `/review` does a final safety pass.

Chain quality rule: `min(q_meta, q_rmp, q_review)` — weakest link gates the output.

questions.prompt: Which single CLI command registers an MCP server with Claude Code?
questions.explanation: `claude mcp add <name> -- <launch-command>` — it writes an entry into `~/.claude.json`'s `mcpServers` map and records the launch line Claude Code should spawn on startup. There is no separate config-editing step.

questions.prompt: In `docs/EXAMPLES.md` §1, the `/chain` quality rule is `min(q_meta, q_rmp, q_review)`. Why is that the right gate for a client-integrated MCP server too?
questions.explanation: A sequential pipeline is only as good as its weakest stage. For an MCP-integrated chain, any failing leg (server unreachable, handshake error, tool rejection) should short-circuit the whole compose; `min(...)` is the quality semantic that enforces exactly that. EXAMPLES.md line 19 spells this out: `weakest link gates the output`.

questions.prompt: Claude Code completed `initialize` but no tools appear when the model asks for them. Which of these is NOT a plausible cause?
questions.explanation: The transport is fine — `initialize` already confirmed that. The three real causes are: (a) the server's `capabilities` bag omits `tools`, (b) `ListToolsRequestSchema` was never registered, (c) the handler returns an empty array. `~/.claude.json` being missing would have prevented the handshake from happening at all.
